narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, we know how much heart and determination it takes to pursue this path, and we're here to help you understand the automated decision on your application in a clear way. Your profile includes an undergraduate GPA of 2.90, which is a bit below the typical 3.21 average for students in our dataset; LSAT score of 25, lower than the usual 36.70; rankings in the 2nd decile for both your first-year and third-year school grades, compared to average deciles around 5-6; a middle family income bracket; and plans to attend full-time, which is the norm for over 90% of applicants. The model's assessment gave you a 40% predicted probability of failing the bar exam on your first try—a threshold that led to your application's rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting those with a stronger forecasted chance of passing to ensure success for everyone. The most influential factor pulling in a positive direction—toward predicting you'd pass—was your undergraduate GPA, which stood out as a relative strength despite being below average. Next in impact was your third-year decile ranking, which unfortunately pushed the prediction toward failure because it was in the lower 2nd decile, signaling challenges in that phase of your schooling compared to peers. Your middle family income also helped by nudging the odds toward passing, aligning with patterns seen in many successful students. Being full-time and your first-year decile had smaller but still favorable influences toward passing. Overall, while some elements like your GPA and income showed promise, the lower third-year performance was the standout concern that tipped the scales toward this outcome, especially when stacked against typical students who often have higher decile rankings and test scores. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many in similar circumstances have improved with targeted effort. We recommend retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your recent academic record through additional coursework or post-bac programs, and perhaps exploring resources tailored for Black women in law like affinity groups or mentorships to build a standout reapplication. You're capable of turning this around, and we're rooting for your next steps.
